analysis:
- Common pipeline across the stronger heuristics: Pareto prefilter → cheap per-point proxy (box-volume ± scalarization) → adaptive shortlist → greedy/CELF lazy-greedy using exact HV on shortlist → bounded 1-for-1 swap cleanup. This balances search quality vs expensive HV calls.
- Effective time/quality levers observed:
  - Shortlist sizing: proportional to k and/or Pareto size; small shortlists cut HV calls but can miss diversity.
  - CELF lazy-greedy + cached singleton HVs: drastically reduces repeated exact HV evaluations versus naive re-evaluation.
  - Pairwise HV caches help only if shortlist ≲ ~30–40; otherwise O(s^2) cost outweighs benefit.
  - Local swaps improve final HV but must be tightly budgeted; many heuristics cap swaps deterministically.
- Avoidable overheads in weaker examples:
  - Repeated full-set HV recomputations inside tight loops (GSI_LS style), rebuilding arrays each iteration, and large numbers of weight vectors (200+) that increase memory and compute without proportionate gain.
  - Unvectorized dominance checks and uncontrolled randomness causing non‑reproducible behavior or extra variance.
  - Large or poorly tuned shortlist/pairwise caches that blow up CPU/time.
- Data reuse & implementation notes:
  - Cache singleton HVs always; reuse hv_selected and update incrementally when possible instead of recomputing from scratch.
  - Use cheap upper bounds (rect-volume, singleton HV) to prune candidates before exact HV calls.
  - Vectorize dominance and proxy-score computations with numpy; limit Python-level loops over points.
  - Keep deterministic tie-breaking and small, fixed RNG seeds for reproducibility.
- Design tradeoff summary: maximize HV per exact-call budget. Strong heuristics minimize HV calls via good proxies, CELF timestamps, and small targeted swaps; weak ones perform many redundant HV computations or use overly expensive proxies.
experience: Keep Pareto prefilter, rank by cheap proxies, run CELF with cached singleton HVs, cap pairwise caching to ≤40, size shortlist ≈ c·k (adaptive with Pareto size), prune with fast upper bounds, update hv_selected incrementally, and bound swap iterations. Vectorize dominance/score steps and avoid huge weight sets—use few weights for ranking then exact HV on shortlist.
current_reflection: Keep Pareto prefilter, rank by cheap proxies, run CELF with cached singleton HVs, cap pairwise caching to ≤40, size shortlist ≈ c·k (adaptive with Pareto size), prune with fast upper bounds, update hv_selected incrementally, and bound swap iterations. Vectorize dominance/score steps and avoid huge weight sets—use few weights for ranking then exact HV on shortlist.
good_reflections: Prefilter dominated points, approximate HVC with few weight vectors to shortlist candidates, then perform a local search using cached incremental HVC deltas. Minimize Python-side HV calls by batching and avoid nested HV recomputations. Make the algorithm deterministic (seeded) and ensure it always returns exactly k points.
bad_reflections: Prefer CELF with cached singletons, adaptive shortlist size (function of Pareto size and k), and conservative pairwise caching only when shortlist ≲ 40. Use cheap upper bounds to prune exact hv calls, incremental hv updates for swaps, and parallelize independent hv evaluations. Tune swap budget and shortlist adaptively to maximize hypervolume per hv-call.

Favor CELF lazy-greedy with singleton (and small pairwise) caches, rank candidates via a hybrid box+weight HVC proxy, choose shortlist size adaptively (f(k, pareto_size)), vectorize Pareto filtering, cap swap checks, and validate only top candidates with exact HV. These choices cut HV calls, keep runtime predictable, and retain high hypervolume quality.

Design shortlists adaptively (scale with Pareto size and k), use cheap HVC proxies to prioritize, run CELF lazy-greedy with singleton+selective pairwise caches, and limit swaps to high-potential candidates. Add incremental HV updates or parallel HV evaluation, stronger upper bounds to prune, and avoid Python-side membership in hot loops. Keep determinism and measure HV-call budget versus shortlist growth.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: Pareto prefilter; CELF lazy-greedy; cached singleton HV; pairwise cache ≤40; few weight HVC proxies; adaptive shortlist ≈ c·k; incremental HV updates; vectorized dominance; swap budget; deterministic.
- Advice: Prefilter dominated points, rank with a few weight-based HVC proxies, build an adaptive shortlist (~c·k by Pareto size), run CELF with cached singletons, allow pairwise caching only when shortlist ≤40, prune with cheap upper bounds, update hv_selected incrementally, cap swaps, vectorize hot loops, seed RNGs; always return exactly k points.
- Avoid: Avoid large weight sets, nested or Python-looped HV recomputations, unbounded pairwise caches, unlimited swap searches, and nondeterministic behavior.
- Explanation: Minimizes costly exact HV calls, keeps runtime predictable, preserves high hypervolume by validating a small shortlist, and yields scalable reproducible local search.
